[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The image?  That's the thirteenth card in The Sacred Geometry Oracle. Card 13, The Octagram, when upright lets you know that other people or factors in the situation can help you pursue your goals; when reversed, it warns you that you're going to have to compromise, and settle for less than everything you want. The waves on the sides of the image tell you that this card belongs to the second third of the oracle, which corresponds to Gwyar, the principle of fluidity and change.

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

A while back, I asked whether there was something which incorporated the ideas of 20th century physics, in particular black holes, into an occult philosophy. You said no, and suggested someone could work on it, and so I took on the project, and would now like to report in on it. The gist of it is that this task will not work well, for the simple reason that black holes, and indeed most 20th century physics, is breathtakingly Plutonian, and thus not suited to working well with occult philosophy.

Black holes are predicted to exist under relativity, and mark a region of space so dense that they create a gravitational pull that not even light can escape from. This creates a division between the outside and inside (the event horizon), and as with most Plutonian phenomena the energies involved are breathtakingly intense (literally infinite: at their heart sits a singularity, a point of infinite density, infinite gravity, and thus infinite energy). Further, the exact behaviours of black holes are unclear: given nothing can ever actually escape them once inside (a strong Plutonian theme itself, an eternal unbreakable prison), observations have been entirely indirect (which also fits the Plutonian theme), and they are a nightmare from a theoretical perspective, which helps reveal the nature of 20th century physics as a Plutonian phenomena: both quantum physics and relativity need to be taken into account in order to make predictions about black holes, and general relativity requires something occurs which quantum physics says is impossible.

To make a long story short, black holes, according to relativity, release energy from their event horizon at a frequency determined solely by the mass, charge, and spin, of the black hole, without regards for anything which it swallowed up. Quantum physics says this is impossible: information contained in the material swallowed by the black hole cannot be erased, as would be the case if relativity is correct and there is no relationship between the material swallowed by a black hole and the radiation it emits.

The thing which strikes me about this is that both the core paradigmatic theories of 20th century physics came into being in the early 20th century, both were subject to massive hype and excitement, and both proved unable to deliver. Furthermore, relativity is tied intimately with nuclear fission and fusion, and divides the former unity of space and time by making them dependent on motion.

Quantum physics, meanwhile, has been subject to hype and failed promises ever since it was invented, with one of the most recent set of hype being around quantum computing; it also divides former unities, in this case stating that small particles can be in more than one place and thus dividing the former unity of position.

Meanwhile, every single attempt to reconcile them has failed, leaving modern physics divided into two distinct, incompatible and contradictory systems. The only place where both need to be taken into account are black holes, and here they reveal they are quite contradictory.

This seems to suggest to me that sometime in the next 15 years or so, we're going to see a major revolution in physics, one which will utterly transform our understanding of the universe and replaces both quantum physics and relativity, much as relativity replaced Classical Mechanics in the early 20th century. I'd also wager that such a replacement would include a disproof of black holes, although the details are well beyond me.

comments.content:
JMG,

Does magic offer any tools for helping one deal with difficult life circumstances and emotional events, such as the death of loved ones? How does the magician confront the inevitability of one's death with dispassion and fortitude?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, magic offers such tools, though it takes time and practice to put them to practical use. In the meantime, occult philosophy may help.

One of the basic lessons of magic is that your material body is the least important part of you. You can learn that by having out-of-body experiences, which will teach you in no uncertain terms that you can exist apart from your material body, or by remembering your own past lives, or by way of various other experiences. Once you grasp this death is no longer a big deal. Shedding your material body is only a little more significant an event than taking off your clothes; you have had other bodies before this one, and you will have other bodies after this one -- and the same is true of everyone else, including the people you care about.

Occult philosophy teaches this. Magic gives you the tools to experience it directly while still in this life. Both can make things much easier to face.

comments.title: Better Scrying, Creative Ritual, Materialist Hangups, Appropriate Seriousness, Occult Dead Ends
comments.content:
Good evening,

Thanks as always for this wonderful resource and for maintaining rules that have created a civil, engaging community. From your own descriptions of setting out on the occult path via books and correspondence courses, I am all too aware what a luxury a resource like this is.

First off, a bit of a shout out: causticus has put together the "Sane Polytheism" community (in homage to Dion Fortune's Sane Occultism). There's been some quite interesting discussions, so for anyone interested in talking with other polytheistic practitioners who are also interested in the more occult side of things, check it out here: https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/

As for questions:

1) For JMG, but thoughts from the commentariat most welcome: I've now had a couple of scrying experiences, and I'm wondering how to be more "perceptive" and less "inventive", if that makes sense. In other words, I would like to be open to whatever imaginal experience I might encounter, but I'm not sure how to stop myself from just steering things wherever I might want them to go. Do you have any thoughts besides "more practice, and keep trying to hold that intention"?

2) I was listening to an interview with a writer, and he talked about how he has struggled to do what some writers do, which is to set aside a specific block of time everyday for writing. He further said that he knew that for many writers who successfully kept that habit, making it "sacred" and a "ritual" was a part of the success. This made me wonder: would it potentially be valuable to come up with a Grove- or Lodge-like ritual to create a space in which to do one's chosen creative work?

3) In your experience, for someone raised in a typical modern materialist-myth-of-progress worldview who has started down an occult path, are there any particularly stubborn notions/ways of thinking/concepts/paradigms/etc that are especially hard to let go of/get past? If so, any particular antidotes besides more daily ritual/meditation/divination?

4) A bit of a broad question, so I understand if nothing much comes to mind: anything that most new/aspiring occultists take too seriously? Anything they don't take seriously enough?

5) Are there any avenues of occult investigation that you have become interested in, looked into, and then decided were dead ends? If so, I'd be interested to hear how often it was a) "not for me", b) worthless, c) not enough evidence, or d) other. Specific examples are welcome, of course, and if any of those categories above had particular warning signs (especially "worthless"!), I'd be thankful to hear about that too.

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Better Scrying, Creative Ritual, Materialist Hangups, Appropriate Seriousness, Occult Dead Ends
replies.content:
1) The only way I know of is more practice -- scrying practice, but also regular meditation, which helps you learn to shift from active to receptive modes and back again.

2) Good question. By all means experiment if that interests you.

3) It really varies from person to person, and so do the appropriate responses.

4) Most beginning occultists take their psychic experiences much more seriously than they should, and take the humdrum routine of daily practice much less seriously than they should.

5) Yes. I looked into chaos magic when it first came out, and decided after study and reflection that it was too watered-down and surrendered too much to the materialist mainstream for my taste; I think that counts as "not for me." I also looked into Traditionalism, and went away shaking my head at its sneering arrogance, posturing, and intellectual fakery. Finally, I've looked into some systems of practice that claimed to be Really Powerful Magic, and backed away when it turned out they had a few beginners-level tricks and nothing more. Manly P. Hall has a great little essay somewhere that points out that common sense is just as valid on the spiritual levels of being as anywhere else; if someone is saying something that would be stupid, hateful, or silly applied to material reality, it keeps that quality when applied to spiritual realities.

replies.title: Re: Better Scrying, Creative Ritual, Materialist Hangups, Appropriate Seriousness, Occult Dead Ends
replies.content:
1) I don't look it, but this isn't entirely a problem I've had. My best suggestion in this case is to "ask" what is being viewed. When you're intentionally imagining what is taking place, I find that there's a brittleness to the sensation of the vision. Sometimes you can get bits and pieces that are sort of what's imagined, and sort of what is from the scrying itself.

There's also how, like in dreams, you don't want to try and warp things, and let them lead you along instead. Of course, you need to keep in mind that this can sometimes backfire, with entities or energies asking you for something really bad.

comments.title: Pluto vs. Monism
comments.content:
One of the things that has occurred to me since last week is that Pluto's influence arrived on the scene shortly after Eastern spirituality, including Advaita Vedanta -- arguably the most uncompromisingly monist spirituality ever -- hit the world scene, and also shortly before their resurgence in popularity from the 60's until... well, they're arguably fading in influence as we speak.

Then there's politics: the birth of the Soviet Union corresponded with the discovery of Pluto, came onto the scene with a thunderous roar and for a while seemed unstoppable, then finally petered out and fell during Pluto's transit through Scorpio.

In other words, in both cases Pluto may have subverted what would have been Neptunian juggernauts that could have realistically displaced their competition. For all the trouble it's caused, this suggests a positive aspect to Pluto as a temporary balancing factor.

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content: Pluto wasn't a mistake or an accident. He was a necessary factor during his time as a planet, and I don't think he's lost all his usefulness yet!

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content:
Pluto was always Pluto but after discovery, it was declared to be a planet and recently downgraded to dwarf-planet (one of many).

Q - "During his time as a planet..." to me infers something close to numerology. By changing my name I can change my name number which changes the time / date calculations. Humanity recently downgraded Pluto and even though it is still the same we have the power to change it's influence on us?

Take all the stupidity that is "fact" these days and let's say for the sake of argument that at some point, humanity is convinced that Saturn or Neptune are no longer planets because they are not politically correct enough and get de-platformed into whatever crazy category. These most significant plants stay the same as they always have been but humanity or even individuals(?) have the choice to define our relationship with them?

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content:
That's a misunderstanding I corrected in the introduction of my book on the subject. Scientists don't have power over the heavens -- the heavens have power over scientists. The discovery of Pluto in 1930 and the 2006 recognition that it's not a planet were both reflections, in the human world, of changes in celestial influence. In astronomical terms, Pluto never was a planet; it's tiny -- 1/7th the size of our Moon -- and the calculations that led to its discovery turned out to be based on mistaken estimates of the mass of Uranus and Neptune. It was a little lump of ice that happened to be in the right place to be mistaken for a planet -- but that mistake was a function of celestial influences, which is why I say that astrologically, Pluto functioned as a planet from 1930 to 2006, with 30 years of wiggle room on each side.

The thing most people don't realize is that this whole story has happened before. Ceres was thought to be a planet when she was first discovered, and retained that status for 50 years before being downgraded -- and in that period she functioned astrologically as a planet. The Romantic Movement was her effect on the world, just as Modernism was Pluto's effect on the world. Notice also that when Uranus and Neptune were discovered, their influence leapt up in intensity by orders of magnitude. All this goes to show that the discovery and downgrading of planets, real or apparent, is a reflection of the same forces that astrology tracks.

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content: Something I find fascinating is that this misunderstanding gets brought up almost every single time the discussion turns towards Pluto being downgraded, almost everywhere I've see these discussions happen, and it is the most Plutonian way to misunderstand what is being said too...

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content: Yes, but it's also hardwired into the habitual thinking of our culture. It's a dogma in modern life that human beings always have the active role and everything else in the cosmos just sits there and takes whatever we dish out. The thought that we might be being shaped, directed, and influenced by nonhuman forces isn't something most people are even willing to think about.

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content: Isn't this entire attitude itself absurdly Plutonian though? So, with any luck, it should go away soon, right?

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content: I hope so!

replies.title: Re: Pluto vs. Monism
replies.content:
There are subcultures within modernity that talk about such forces, as long as they're impersonal. Marxists talk about economic conditions determining class consciousnesses, evoking Hegel's quasi-religious cosmology. Post-Marxists talk about power discourses and structural racism creating false consciousness. Mencius Moldbug likened the systemic ratchet effects of democratic legitimation politics to the action of Cthulhu: "Cthulhu may swim slowly. But he only swims left." His part of the alt-right talked about value erosion by demographic effects and the memetic advantages of the short-term happiness of cultural seed-corn-eaters. There was someone suggesting that Yog-Sothoth be identified with the erosive effects of markets specifically. They even used to have an aesthetic tendency called "horrorism", about actively seeking out the feelings of distress and hopelessness and struggle in the face of the seemingly inevitable, at contemplating the destruction of everything of human value by impersonal forces unleashed by modernity. Yudkowsky tried to get his readers to understand what natural selection really was by likening it to Azathoth, as a "blind idiot god" at the center of everything. Scott Alexander discussed the general theme in an article titled after Ephesians 6:12, with imagery of "Vast Formless Things" from a Poe poem:

https://slatestarcodex.com/2013/03/07/we-wrestle-not-with-flesh-and-blood-but-against-powers-and-principalities/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not that I know of, but then I don't keep track of the latest fashionable gimmicks in pop-culture magic.

2) Our brains don't work like computers. That's why computers do such a miserably poor job of imitating brains. I suppose you could use programming as a metaphor -- I think Timothy Leary did that at one point -- but it strikes me as a very clumsy and unhelpful metaphor, much less useful than other ways of thinking about magic.

I'll expand on that a little. The idea of programming takes it as given that you, the programmer, are the active factor; the computer is passive, doing what it's told; and the program is a sequence of logical steps that makes the computer do what you want it to do. Approach the universe that way and you'll always be blindsided, because the rest of the world is not passive and will not do as it's told. Neither will your own mind and body, by the way. Programming as a metaphor for acting in the world is one more expression of the myth of the machine, the delusion that claims that we are the only active and creative forces in a passive, mechanical universe -- and the universe isn't interested in playing along with that dreary fantasy.

3) Nope. You can't do that without controlling all the variables, and since the universe will not sit still -- and neither will your body and mind -- you can't control the variables. So the baseline remains utterly unknown.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) A relative recently went on to me about how we're living in an overly feminine age, this person told me the reason for low fertility nowadays is because of "weak males" giving in to "empowered women". It does raise a question: isn't the overly masculine Arnold Schwarzenegger alpha-male type this relative of mine wants to see return also itself unbalanced?

Is it true that part of balancing the self involves integrating both the masculine and feminine sides of oneself so that you aren't a caricature of either an unbalanced male or female? I'm thinking of some of Dion Fortune's work on polarity.

2) Forgive me if I've misquoted you, but if I remember right, you once said here that an initiatory system based around the Arthurian legends would fail, because the Arthur, in the legends, ended up failing in his goals. There is a section in the Druidry Handbook that draws on the symbolism of Arthur, does this pose any dangers in terms of contemplating it too closely?

3) A basic astrology question: obviously a lot has been written about the planets used in astrology, but what about the Earth herself? Is there a role and astrological symbolism for Earth?

4) In John Keel's book The Mothman Prophecy he writes that maybe some of the UFO phenomenon could be the equivalent of faeries making appearances in modern guise. Do you think that similar appearances may be happening in today's culture (maybe via the internet) and we just don't know about it yet?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Good. One of the classic bad habits of human thought is the notion that if you've got an imbalance one way, you can fix it by going all the way to the opposite and equally unbalanced extreme. There's a middle range where real balance lies -- in this case, men and women who are equally empowered, equally comfortable with their own bodies, and equally able to accept or turn down classic gender roles, and to allow others to make their own choices. If a man wants to do the Schwarzenegger trip, great -- he ought to be able to find a gym close by and a gun range somewhere not too far off -- but I've noticed that a lot of men who obsess about the same talking points as your relative aren't willing to do that. They want to feel more manly by putting down women, rather than by becoming men.

2) The problem comes when you try to reenact the Arthurian mythos in ritual or in real life. There have been a number of crown princes of England named Arthur, for example, but not one of them has lived to become king! In the same way, though not with the same drastic results, there have been plenty of magical lodges that have tried to use Arthurian symbolism as their core mythos, and they reliably fail. You can read Arthurian legends and study Arthurian symbols all you want without risk, it's when you try to enact it that the problems pop up.

3) No, because she's always in the same place -- straight down -- and so her influence doesn't change.

4) Oh, the fays have been all over the internet for years now. I've long since lost track of the number of stories I've heard of people being pixy-led in the classic fashion by following map programs...

comments.title: Sacred Geometry Oracle - interpretation
comments.content:
JMG,

A question about how to interpret a Sacred Geometry Oracle reading, if I may:

We‘re in a personal situation involving our kid and public authorities, where we have several options on how to proceed (and probably quite a few others that we haven‘t considered yet, as is usually the case).

In order to figure out what action (if anything) to take today, I just did a reading and started with:

„What is the best possible outcome for us if

A. We do nothing today
B. We take action today“

Result was:

A. 14. The Dodecagram upright - Completeness
B. 2. The Point upright - Beginning

I intended to ask further questions depending on the outcome of the first two cards (e.g. which specific kind of action to take today), but frankly, at this point, I simply gave up - I have no clue how to interpret this.

B. seems to suggest that if we take action today, the best possible outcome (depending on the kind of action) would be that we start on a process/set a beginning point somewhere that defines the rest of the process. Whether that outcome would be good or bad (or: better or worse than if we did nothing today), that is beyond me.

A. seems to suggest that the situation is going to take care of itself if we do nothing, and potentially (but by far not guaranteed) in a good way - although it’s not one of the cards pointing to outright success, so…

If you would have gotten this reading, would you take that as a signal to take action today, or not, and why?

Or would you ask further questions to clarify (which ones?)?

Or is the problem mainly with the way I put my initial questions (and if so, how would you have approached this reading in order to get a clearer result?)?

a slightly puzzled Milkyway ;-)


PS: Just to clarify, I‘ve only been working with the Oracle for about three weeks, and my results are sometimes like random, sometimes „meh“, and sometimes „wow“, with a tendency to improve. ;-) Also, I have a personal stake in this thing which isn‘t the best basis for a solid reading.

I.e. I‘m not relying on these readings for our actions in this, but take them as nice training grounds and would really appreciate your thoughts in order to learn…

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry Oracle - interpretation
replies.content: Okay, I'd read this in the following way. If you do nothing, things will wind up and that'll be the end of it. If you take action, it's going to be the beginning of a long process. If my goal in the situation was to make the authorities go away and stop bothering my kid, I'd do nothing; if I wanted to push the issue further, knowing that it's going to be a long process, I'd take action.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry Oracle - interpretation
replies.content:
Got it, thank you very much! I wasn‘t sure about the Dodecagram upright - from how I understand it, it doesn‘t necessarily imply success, so that had me confused.

(In practical terms, I‘m afraid this ship has sailed, as we have indeed taken action today - as I said, I don‘t quite trust my divination yet, and this particular action seemed to be the smartest way forward, overall. So I might well be back next week with more interpretation questions… :D )

Thanks a lot - both for the opportunity to ask questions (and get thoughtful replies!) each week, and for the Oracle. I really, really love it and actually have to reign myself in not to overdo it on some days. Now if only meditation would come as easily… ;-)

Milkyway

comments.title: Sharing Astral Space
comments.content:
I was writing in a small bakery by a busy train station, and noticed after a while that the four women scattered about the sitting area were calmly observing me, not even the teenager on her phone. I kept writing, since the space was too cramped to do anything more direct than look at them out of the sides of my eyes. I felt their attention but it was slight, a peaceful regard, like everyone was just chilling there with me, such a rare thing to observe these days.

One middle-aged woman got up to leave. She turned to me and complimented my handwriting, said it was very relaxing to watch me write, and thanked me. I responded in kind and the other women eventually left. When I finished the scene, I was a bit wobbly coming out of it, which reminded me of someone's question on MM regarding a similar sensation coming out of scrying.

Surely there is an occult explanation for this. I was clearly "giving off a vibe" but this seems stronger. Do you think I was sharing some kind of astral space with them? They were all within two meters of me. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Sharing Astral Space
replies.content: Hmm! Keep in mind that we all share each other's astral space anyway -- the astral plane is a continuum, which is why we tend to be influenced by the moods and thoughts of those who are around us. You may have been broadcasting a very pleasant vibe, and they picked it up.

replies.title: Re: Western Medicine and Occult

My reaction was based primarily on a great deal of very bad experience with corporate mainstream medicine. I haven't gotten health care from an MD in more than thirty years -- I've lost too many family members (including my only child) to drug side effects, incompetent medical procedures, and some of the other reasons why medical care is the #3 cause of death in the US today. (More people are killed by physicians every year here than die in car crashes.)

So my immediate response to the vaccines was "Um, no." It was easy enough to find out that they'd received absurdly inadequate testing, and here again, after Fen-Phen, Vioxx, and so many other FDA-approved drugs that killed people, my response was to back away hard. Of course by the time I could have gotten the jab I'd had Covid and shaken it off easily using my preferred alternative healing methods, so I had zero interest in getting an untested experimental drug for it of a type that had never before gotten through human trials without being pulled for severe problems -- and yes, that was easy to find out by then, too.

There may have been some intuition in there, too, but I never did a formal divination on the subject. That would have been like casting a reading to find out if politicians tell lies!

comments.content:
I'm beginning to question how useful master numbers actually are in numerology.

First of all, they've attracted all manner of ridiculous hype -- I ran across a video saying that people with a 33 birth number are all on their last incarnation.

Secondly, the way the math works, a master number 11 is more probable than a regular number 2 for most sums that anyone will actually deal with. For the numbers 1 to 4000, there are 208 numbers that reduce to 2, and 237 that reduce to 11. And the smaller the total sum, the more likely it will sum to 11 rather than 2. The probabilities only turn around at a little over 5000, which is well outside of what most people will be deal with.

replies.title: (no subject)

I find them useful, in that they remind me that a 2 situation can often (though not always) be resolved with a firm decision, and a 4 situation can often (though not always) be accepted and used as an opportunity for reflection and the deepening of one's inner life.

comments.title: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
comments.content:
Hey jmg

Is there any use in using classical myths to understand and work with Neptune and Uranus, or is the only the connection between them the name and therefore the mythology that relates to the new planetary energies is still waiting to be made?

replies.title: Re: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
replies.content:
Uranus and Neptune were named by scientists who didn't take the time to suss out the mythic dimensions of those planets, and so they're badly misnamed. Given their energies, Uranus should probably have been named Prometheus, and Neptune should have been given the name of a Titaness -- Rhea, whose title is "mother of gods," would be my nominee.

Curiously, the minor planets Pluto and Ceres were both much more appropriately named.

replies.title: Re: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
replies.content: So does that mean that classical myths can be used to understand the outer planets, and perhaps get clues on how to work with them?

replies.title: Re: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
replies.content: Depends on the specific myths you have in mind, of course. ("Classical myths" covers a lot of territory.) Starting with a good solid knowledge of the astrological effects of the planets in question would be helpful...

replies.title: Re: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
replies.content: I ask simply because if the outer planets are new and have only just being understood I assume that there wouldn’t be any clues about their nature since the ancients had no awareness of them.

replies.title: Re: The usefulness of myth in understanding the outer planets
replies.content: The outer planets constellate (literally!) archetypal forces that have always existed, but didn't have a defined presence in human society and mentality. You can find myths that echo Uranian and Neptunian themes in ancient times, but they always seem out of focus somehow. So it's a subtle and complex matter to tease those out.

comments.title: Jesus conspiracy?
comments.content:
Hello JMG! Hope you're doing well.

My whole life I believed in the existence of someone called Jesus, who acted as a prophet and "miracle worker" of some kind, as there is historical evidence that such a *man* existed; However I never felt I had any reason to believe the "mythical Jesus," so to speak. So, for me, Jesus "worked" much like Athena, Odin or Cernunnos does. I then came across this whole "Caesar's messiah" conspiracy, where they claim the mythical Jesus was created on top of the historical one by the Flavians, using material from Josephus's narratives about Titus Flavius. Both stories indeed have many striking similarities that show up in the same sequence... I'm tempted to believe this might true, specially as it aligns with what I always more or less believed in. What do you think?

replies.title: Re: Jesus conspiracy?
replies.content: I think that a lot of people have too much time on their hands, and too many tangled emotions around Christianity. I've read several versions of the Flavian theory; by the same logic, and with equal evidence, you could prove that George Washington was Adam Weishaupt -- yes, I know, it's been done -- and that the American Revolution was actually fought on the Moon. The evidence supporting the historical reality of Yeshua bin Maryam, a very minor religious figure in Galilee in the years before the Jewish-Roman war, is about as good as the evidence for any other spiritual or intellectual figure of the time who didn't leave writings of his own behind -- Socrates isn't much better documented, for example -- and the Flavian theory is full of holes that you could march a Roman legion through, starting with the fact that Christians were documented in the Roman world before the Flavian house rose to power.

comments.title: The letter "Z"
comments.content:
This is really a question for Robert Mathiesen.

The Russians use the letter "Z" as a symbol of support for the Russian war effort against Ukraine. Does this letter or symbol have some religious or occult significance?

Also, why "Z"? That´s a Latin letter, but the Russian alphabet is Cyrillic!

At least in Sweden, nobody seems to understand what the letter is supposed to stand for. Is it victory, "the West" (!), or what?

Tidlösa, Sweden

replies.title: Re: The letter "Z"
replies.content: I am not Robert Mathiesen, but here's my theory. Russia and Ukraine use very similarly looking military vehicles (soviet legacy), so the Russian Army needed to find a way to identify friends and foes on the battlefield; marking their troops with the "Z" symbol was their solution. As to why the letter "Z" was used - I have no idea. One explanation I heard is that it stands for "Za Pobedu" - "To victory" in Russian. I would take it with a grain of salt. I don't think there's any occult meaning to this, but maybe Putin wants to say that this is "ze end" of the old world order? Or it may not be a letter at all, but a symbol, like a lightning bolt.

replies.title: Re: The letter "Z"
replies.content: I don't know for certain, but Russian tanks and other vehicles in a given unit seem to have been marked with a variety of straight and curved lines as identifiers.  A few of these devices resemble Latin-alphabet letters (Z, V); others do not.  None of them that I have seen look like Cyrillic letters at all, and the Cyrillic alphabet is the one that Russian troops would know best.  So they're probably random unit identifiers, and don't stand for anything--or didn't stand for anything originally.  (Z now seems to have been repurposed up as a pro-Russian symbol.  I haven't seen any explanation of why it was chosen rather than some other unit identifier.)

My translation of Etteilla's Book of Thoth, coming out in April, has a method of dream interpretation using the Book of Thoth tarot cards (and a worked example). You could probably do it with any tarot pack. comments.title: NPCs
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This is a semi-occulty thing, but I have come across repeated articles (often on more esoteric matters, interestingly, but also, increasingly, on conspiracy/alternative narrative forums) where there have been references to segments of the human population as 'NPCs', who essentially just occupy space and 'make up the numbers'. If you don't know already, this is a term from video gaming which stands for "non-player character", and typically refers to computer-controlled characters who may assist you on missions, act as nondescript enemies or are otherwise just bystanders. This feels like a very worrying turn of events to me, as it has echoes of the first dehumanising step in mass murder, where people are equated with animals. In some ways, this is more alarming, as NPCs are often literally just there as cannon fodder in some games such as Grand Theft Auto.

It's slipping into the corona counter-narrative as well, where those who lined up to get the shots are NPCs that can be expected to die imminently and are of no consequence.

Yes, some people probably do follow the herd far more blindly, but they are probably less advanced on their spiritual journey. However, it did make me wonder if human bodies ever just get filled by random souls (not just animals) of typically non-corporeal entities, who may have a hard time adjusting to the physical constraints of limbs?

And final thing - have you seen any more evidence of whether the whole corona business is divine wrath, or demonic shenanigans as yet? (Apologies if I missed it, I've had to take a break from the open posts for a few weeks for my own well-being, as I was reading them compulsively)
Thanks.

replies.title: Re: NPCs
replies.content:
Okay, now you're making me feel old. Long ages ago, when dinosaurs roamed the earth and Pac-Man was on the cutting edge of video game technology, we already knew about NPCs. The term was first used in roleplaying games of the Dungeons & Dragons variety for the characters who were controlled by the dungeon master, rather than by the other players, and prepackaged adventures and dungeons typically came with a set of NPCs for the DM to have fun with.

Yes, I've also seen the term NPC used in alt-right circles, complete with an image:
[IMAGE:0]

There are no NPCs in the real world. There's always a soul in there somewhere, though it can be hard to find! There are certainly people whose state of consciousness is not that different from a shallow doze, and who believe whatever authority figures backed by peer pressure tell them. There are also human bodies inhabited by souls that probably don't belong there -- animal souls, to begin with, but in some cases also the souls of elementals and other usually disembodied beings.

As for your final question, I'm waiting to see what happens now that the push to vaccinate is over in most places. The data's not in yet.
